sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Pushes must occur A before B before C, but an item may be popped before later pushes. ABC is possible by pushing and immediately popping each item. CBA is possible after pushing all three. BAC is possible by push A, push B, pop B, pop A, then push/pop C. CAB is impossible: making C the first pop requires all three pushes to have occurred, leaving C above B above A, so B must pop before A.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Explicit legal operation sequences exist for ABC, CBA, and BAC. Only CAB conflicts with the forced LIFO order after C is popped first.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Once C is first to pop, all three pushes have occurred and the remaining B-before-A order is forced.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A binary heap supports insertion and removal of the highest/lowest-priority element in O(log n), while inspecting that element is O(1). It is compact and is the standard efficient priority-queue implementation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: An unsorted array/list makes deletion of the best element linear; a sorted list makes insertion linear. An arbitrary binary tree gives no priority guarantee, whereas a heap is the specifically constrained tree needed.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Heap order keeps the extreme-priority item at the root while preserving logarithmic updates.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A complete binary tree with n nodes has height h=⌊log₂n⌋. The longest path can run from a deepest leaf through the root to a deepest leaf in the opposite subtree, using about 2h edges. Hence the diameter is about 2log₂n.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: log₂n estimates root-to-leaf height, only half the possible leaf-to-leaf route. The alternatives involving n are far too large for a logarithmic-height tree.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Tree diameter is at most twice its height and is nearly that value for a complete tree.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Separate chaining stores colliding entries in an auxiliary chain at each array index. A bucket can therefore hold arbitrarily many entries, limited only by available memory rather than the 100 array slots. There is no fixed upper limit from table size alone.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 100 is the bound for open addressing, not chaining. 200 and 10,000 have no basis without a separate memory/load-factor constraint.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Chaining decouples entry count from the number of hash buckets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For order 5, a node holds at most four keys. Insert 7,8,1,4; inserting 13 overflows [1,4,7,8,13], so promote 7, leaving [1,4] and [8,13]. Insert 20 on the right. Insert 2,6 on the left; inserting 5 overflows [1,2,4,5,6], so promote 4. The root becomes [4,7], containing two elements.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Counts 1,3,4 result from omitting the second split, promoting the wrong key, or confusing key count with child count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: An order-m B-tree has at most m children and m−1 keys per node; promote the median on overflow.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: An SNMP response PDU uses the error-status field to state whether an error occurred and what class it belongs to. If relevant, error-index identifies the variable binding associated with that error.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Error-index locates an offending binding but does not itself report the error type. Set-request is a PDU operation, and agent-index is not the required response field.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: error-status says what happened; error-index says where in the variable list.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A URL identifies the retrieval scheme/method (such as http), the host name, and the path to the document. A port may be included but can be omitted when the scheme's default applies. DNS is a resolution service used to map the host; it is not a component that must appear in the URL. Thus I,II,IV.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options A and B include DNS as a URL component, while D omits the host/path and includes optional or external mechanisms.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: URL structure is scheme://host[:port]/path; DNS operates behind the name.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The transport layer provides logical end-to-end, process-to-process communication between applications on the two final hosts, including segmentation, reliability/flow control when supported, and port addressing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: The network layer provides host-to-host packet routing; data link is hop-to-hop; session manages dialogs above transport.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Transport is the OSI end-to-end process-delivery layer.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The Internet layer supplies logical addressing and routing, and its IP header includes service/traffic-class mechanisms used to request differentiated quality of service. It does not guarantee connection-oriented delivery, and framing belongs to the link/network-access layer. Therefore 1,2,3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options B–D include connection-oriented delivery, framing, or both, which are not Internet-layer IP services.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: IP provides addressing and routing with best-effort service; frames belong below it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The original IEEE 802.11 infrared physical layer specified data rates of 1 and 2 Mbit/s. Its maximum operating rate is therefore 2 Mbit/s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 1 Mbit/s is the lower supported rate; 5 and 11 Mbit/s are not rates of that original infrared PHY (11 Mbit/s is associated with 802.11b radio).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Legacy 802.11 infrared supported 1 or 2 Mbit/s.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: An absolute loader's task is deliberately simple: it reads absolute object code and places it into the exact memory addresses already specified. Thus the function actually performed by the loader is loading.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Allocation, linking, and relocation/address assignment must have been resolved before the absolute loader runs, by the programmer/translator toolchain.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Absolute loader = copy object words to predetermined addresses.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The parse-tree root is multiplication. Its left child is a plus node with leaves A and B; its right child is C. Reading children recursively gives (A+B)*C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: C omits the grouping and would root the tree at +. B is malformed prefix/infix notation, and D pairs different operands under multiplication.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Parenthesize each internal parse-tree node around the expressions represented by its children.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.questionText: Consider left-associative operators in decreasing precedence: − (subtraction), * (multiplication), and $ (exponentiation). Evaluate 3−2*4 $ 1*2 $ 3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Apply the unusual stated precedence, not ordinary arithmetic. Highest: 3−2=1. Next, multiplication forms 1*4=4 and 1*2=2. Lowest, the two left-associative exponentiations give (4 $ 2) $ 3=(4²)³=16³=4096.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 64 and 512 come from different exponent associativity/grouping; −61 results from applying familiar precedence rather than the explicitly supplied order. The scan renders the second $ poorly, but 4096 and the declared operator set determine the intended expression.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: First group by declared precedence, then use left associativity within each level.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: In the Chomsky hierarchy, regular grammars are contained in context-free grammars, which are contained in context-sensitive grammars. Among the listed phrase-structure classes, context-sensitive grammar is therefore the most general.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Regular and context-free grammars generate strictly narrower language classes. 'None' is false because context-sensitive is offered.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Regular ⊂ context-free ⊂ context-sensitive ⊂ unrestricted.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The lexical analyzer, also called a scanner, reads the character stream and groups characters into lexemes, emitting tokens such as identifiers, keywords, and numeric literals.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: A parser groups tokens into grammatical structures; optimization and code generation occur later and do not perform lexical grouping.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Characters→scanner→tokens→parser→syntax structure.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Disk scheduling uses SCAN (A-2). Traditional batch jobs are served FIFO (B-4). Time-sharing CPU service uses round-robin (C-1). Nested interrupt servicing follows a LIFO return/priority stack (D-3). Thus 2,4,1,3.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Other mappings attach CPU round-robin to disk/interrupt work or swap the standard FIFO, SCAN, and LIFO roles.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: SCAN→disk, FIFO→batch, RR→time sharing, LIFO→nested interrupts.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A semaphore is the synchronization object that controls concurrent access to critical resources. Its atomic wait/P and signal/V operations coordinate processes and can enforce mutual exclusion and resource-count limits.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: P and V are operations on the semaphore, not the complete synchronization object. Swapping is memory/process management and does not protect a critical resource. Also, semaphores prevent races; avoiding deadlock still requires correct acquisition design.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Semaphore state plus atomic P/V operations regulate access to shared resources.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Round-robin is inherently preemptive: a timer interrupts a running process at the end of its quantum and returns it to the ready queue if unfinished.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Shortest-job/seek-first variants are not inherently preemptive, and priority scheduling may be implemented either preemptively or nonpreemptively, making it less uniquely correct.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: The time quantum is the explicit preemption mechanism in round-robin.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A binary semaphore used as a mutex is initialized to 1, representing one available permit. The first process performs wait and changes it to 0; others then block until the owner signals it back to 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Starting at 0 blocks every process initially, while 2 or 3 would permit multiple entrants and violate mutual exclusion.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Mutex semaphore: 1 means free, 0 means occupied.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A remote computing service lets a user interact with a distant computer while its resources are shared among users by time slicing. It therefore combines time sharing with interactive processing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Batch processing lacks the interactive remote dialogue; multiprocessing is an implementation possibility rather than the defining service mode; real-time processing imposes deadline constraints not implied here.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Remote time-sharing provides an interactive terminal session on a shared host.
